title: Chapter 49: The First Seat Falls
content: The First Seat stood before the closed Hell Gate, its form flickering with instability.
"You have sealed the gate. But you have not defeated me."
"I'm not trying to defeat you. I'm trying to replace you."
"Replace me?"
"The Council needs a First Seat. But it doesn't have to be you."
The First Seat's form wavered.
"You cannot remove me. I am the First Seat. I have been the First Seat since the Council's founding."
"And now you're not."
"On what authority?"
"On the authority of the restored Primordial Void. The original contract. The system that predates your seat and will outlast it."
She held up her empty hand.
"I call a vote. All Council members present. Shall the First Seat be removed for violation of the original contract?"
The Seventh Seat's light appeared beside her. "In favor."
The Sixth Seat's smoke spiral materialized. "In favor."
The Fifth Seat's stone form emerged from the shadows. "In favor."
Three votes.
The First Seat looked at the remaining members. But they were not there. Only the three who had turned against it.
"You have no quorum," the First Seat said. "The motion fails."
"Actually, I counted wrong," Roxanne said. "There are four of us."
She raised her hand.
"The heir of the Primordial Void votes. In favor."
"You cannot vote —"
"The original contract gives the Void's heir a permanent seat on any council that derives its authority from the Void. And this one does."
She smiled.
"Four in favor. Zero against. The motion carries. The First Seat is removed."
The First Seat's form dissolved.
Not into nothing. Into something smaller. Older. A figure that had been hiding behind the power for forty thousand years.
It was a creature. Bent. Old. Dressed in the robes of a bygone era. Its thousand eyes were gone. Its presence was gone. It was just... a thing. Sitting on the ground.
"I was there at the beginning," it said quietly. "I helped build the Exchange. I thought I was protecting order. But somewhere along the way, I started protecting myself."
Roxanne looked at the fallen Primordial.
"Get up."
"What?"
"Get up. You're not banished. You're not destroyed. You're retired. Find something else to do."
"What would I do?"
"I don't know. Open a shop. Write a book. Learn to make coffee. There's a whole world out here that doesn't run on debt."
The former First Seat stared at her.
"You are letting me live."
"I'm letting you choose. That's the difference between your system and mine."
She turned and walked back toward the surface.
Behind her, the old creature sat in the crimson light, staring at its empty hands, trying to imagine what it would do with them now.
